abstract: Implementing efficient carbon dioxide reduction into value-added chemicals requires the development of catalytic materials that are selective, stable, and composed of earth-abundant elements. In this study, we demonstrate that nanostructured thin films of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) serve as powerful, atomically defined platforms for the electrocatalytic reduction of CO₂ to CO in aqueous media. By systematically screening various metal-porphyrin building blocks integrated into an aluminum-based framework, we identified the cobalt-functionalized MOF, Al₂(OH)₂TCPP-Co, as an exceptionally effective catalyst. Optimized MOF thin films, fabricated via atomic layer deposition-mediated synthesis, achieved a Faradaic efficiency for CO production of 76% and sustained stability for over seven hours with a per-site turnover number (TON) of 1400. In situ spectroelectrochemical analysis revealed that the majority of the cobalt active sites within the porous landscape are redox-accessible, undergoing a Co(II) to Co(I) reduction that drives the catalytic cycle. This work establishes MOFs as a versatile class of heterogeneous electrocatalysts that bridge the gap between molecular precision and robust material architecture for sustainable energy applications.
